response: Alex has always had a strong belief in traditional values, especially when it comes to love and relationships. This month, Alex decided to challenge those beliefs by reading novels that present different views. The first novel Alex read was a short but impactful story. It had 120 pages and took Alex four days to finish. Each day, Alex read the same number of pages. This helped Alex understand how much time was spent on each book. The second novel was longer, with 180 pages. It took Alex six days to complete. The pages were more complex, requiring more time to process. Alex read the same number of pages every day, maintaining consistency. The third novel was the longest, with 240 pages. It took Alex eight days to finish. Each day, Alex read the same amount of pages. This pattern helped Alex track progress. The first novel had the fewest pages, so the daily reading was the least. The second novel had more pages, so the daily reading increased. The third novel had the most pages, so the daily reading was the highest. Alex read the same number of pages each day for all three novels. This consistency made it easier to compare the reading pace. The first novel had 120 pages over four days, so the daily pages were 30. The second novel had 180 pages over six days, so the daily pages were 30. The third novel had 240 pages over eight days, so the daily pages were 30. Alex read 30 pages each day for all three novels. This means the daily reading was the same for each book. The total pages read across all three novels were 120 plus 180 plus 240. That adds up to 540 pages. The total days spent reading were four plus six plus eight. That totals 18 days. To find the average pages per day, Alex divided the total pages by the total days. The average was 540 divided by 18. This equals 30 pages per day. The average pages per day across all three novels was 30. Alex read 30 pages each day, regardless of the book. This consistency helped Alex maintain a steady reading pace.
